content.text:
**Todo #1 is in place** — *"sync: reposition @ onto the synced bookmark after a successful apply"* — and the list validates clean (10 entries, all sequential).

The entry captures everything we settled on:
- **Apply-mode only**, final pass after all repos sync successfully; `--check` never mutates `@`.
- **Code repo** decision tree keyed on `@-` vs `xxx` (proper-descendant → `jj new` when `@` empty, gated `--rebase` when not; `xxx == @-` no-op; diverged → inform).
- **`.claude`**: `@-` on `main` → `jj new main`, else error.
- The two recommended calls I made (bool `--rebase`; reposition errors sit *outside* the `op_restore` revert region).
- Plumbing note that the role is collapsed to `Vec<PathBuf>` by the time `sync_repos` runs, so the bot repo has to be re-derived from `other-repo`.

Nothing is implemented yet — this is queued as the top priority for when you want to open a cycle on it.

message.content: Let's open a cycle on Todo #1

content.text: Opening a cycle requires the protocol. Let me read it, and check the current version to pick the next one.

content.content:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form).
4	
5	## Cycles
6	
7	A cycle has three phases:
8	
9	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
10	  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
11	  - Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.
12	  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
13	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
14	    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
15	    statement + plan ladder).
16	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
17	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
18	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
19	  needs; each runs through the
20	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
21	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
22	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
23	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
24	    `## Done`.
25	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
26	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
27	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
28	    changed.
29	
30	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
31	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
32	result must always be published at close-out. See
33	[Pushing](#pushing).
34	
35	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
36	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
37	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
38	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
39	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
40	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
41	for the local-ladder mechanics.
42	
43	## Chores sections
44	
45	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
46	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle:
47	
48	- One section per cycle is the default.
49	- Per-commit sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a
50	  deliberate alternative.
51	
52	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
53	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
54	with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
55	`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
56	content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).
57	
58	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
59	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
60	AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).
61	
62	## Preparation
63	
64	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
65	
66	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
67	  `Commits:` ref** — see
68	  [Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references).
69	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
70	- **Update `Cargo.lock`** to match — run `cargo build`
71	  (or `cargo check`) so the lockfile's `vc-x1` version
72	  tracks `Cargo.toml` in the same commit.
73	- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
74	  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
75	  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
76	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
77	  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
78	  - A **plan ladder**.
79	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
80	  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
81	  close-out title.
82	
83	## Work-N
84	
85	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
86	implement the change. As many as needed:
87	
88	- Each commit runs through the
89	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
90	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
91	  visibility).
92	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
93	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
94	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
95	  scope grows enough (see
96	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
97	
98	## Close-out
99	
100	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
101	only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
102	what happens post-squash:
103	
104	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
105	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
106	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
107	  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
108	  placeholder opened during Preparation):
109	  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
110	    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
111	    chores `##` header already carries that string).
112	  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
113	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
114	    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
115	    back-reference.
116	  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
117	    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
118	  - Replace `## In Progress` with
119	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
120	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
121	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
122	
123	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
124	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
125	see [Pushing](#pushing).
126	
127	## Numbering
128	
129	The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase —
130	the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:
131	
132	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
133	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
134	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
135	
136	Disambiguation:
137	
138	- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
139	  Preparation.
140	- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
141	  identifier `0`).
142	
143	**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:
144	
145	- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
146	- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
147	- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
148	- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle
149	
150	Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
151	`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
152	`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.
153	
154	Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
155	reports the active phase at build time.
156	
157	## Per-commit flow
158	
159	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
160	through:
161	
162	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
163	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
164	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
165	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
166	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
167	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
168	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
169	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
170	   1. `cargo fmt`
171	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
172	   3. `cargo test`
173	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
174	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
175	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
176	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
177	   changes and we iterate until complete.
178	6. **Write the commit description** — see
179	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
180	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
181	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
182	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
183	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
184	   keeps the verb visible):
185	
186	   ```
187	   jj commit -m \
188	   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
189	   -m "<intro paragraph>
190	
191	   - file1: gist
192	   - file2: gist
193	
194	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
195	   -R .
196	   ```
197	
198	**Two overrides apply:**
199	
200	- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
201	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
202	  surface it; don't push through.
203	- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
204	  a review or question forward.
205	
206	## Commit description
207	
208	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)
209	with a version suffix:
210	
211	```
212	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
213	```
214	
215	### Title
216	
217	- ≤50 chars total (version suffix counts).
218	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
219	  `docs`, `chore`.
220	- Favor terse phrasings.
221	
222	### Body
223	
224	[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
225	wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:
226	
227	- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
228	  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
229	  files with multiple distinct changes:
230	
231	  ```
232	  - path/to/file1
233	    - first distinct change
234	    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
235	       with continuation indented 5
236	  ```
237	
238	  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
239	  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
240	  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
241	  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.
242	
243	- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
244	  in-session activity rather than code changes.
245	
246	### Trailer
247	
248	`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
249	[ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).
250	
251	## Reviewing changes
252	
253	Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
254	on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
255	editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:
256	
257	- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
258	- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
259	- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
260	- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev
261	
262	Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
263	it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
264	`jj show -r @-`.
265	
266	See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
267	close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
268	are in [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).
269	
270	## Pushing
271	
272	### Policy
273	
274	Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
275	progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
276	the cycle's result must be published.
277	
278	### Shape at close-out push
279	
280	At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
281	shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
282	the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
283	the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
284	needs approval), so choose deliberately.
285	
286	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
287	  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
288	  focused on one or two files.
289	- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
290	  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
291	  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
292	  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
293	  for the full setup sequence.
294	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
295	  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
296	  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
297	  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.
298	
299	Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
300	`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.
301	
302	### Merge non-ff recipe
303	
304	Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
305	close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
306	close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
307	(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
308	Work commit:
309	
310	1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
311	   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
312	   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
313	   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
314	   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.
315	
316	   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
317	   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
318	2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
319	   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
320	   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
321	   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
322	3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.
323	
324	**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
325	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
326	immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
327	push force-updates `main`.
328	The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
329	the close-out push.
330	
331	### vc-x1 push wrapper
332	
333	`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
334	`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.
335	
336	`vc-x1 push` injects the `ochid:` trailers itself (the
337	`commit-app` / `commit-claude` stages append them) — don't
338	hand-write them into the commit body or `--title`/`--body`.
339	
340	**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
341	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
342	shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
343	planned:
344	
345	- N:1 code↔bot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
346	- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
347	- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).
348	
349	### .claude cadence
350	
351	**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
352	with every code commit in that push.
353	
354	The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
355	across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
356	snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
357	code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.
358	
359	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
360	on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
361	create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
362	app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
363	pushes to the wrong remote ref.
364	
365	Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
366	session information created, which we don't worry about.
367	The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
368	remain empty — this behavior was verified in 0.64.0.
369	
370	### Bot communication at the reviews
371	
372	Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
373	"Checkpoint N", etc.):
374	
375	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
376	  "Work complete. Please review."
377	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
378	  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
379	  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
380	  --body ...` invocation by default.
381	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
382	  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
383	  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
384	  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.
385	
386	### After push or finalize: stop and wait
387	
388	After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
389	**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
390	.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
391	hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
392	proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
393	user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
394	step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
395	stop for the whole turn.
396	
397	`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
398	then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
399	no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
400	closing words *before* invoking it; do not purposely emit anything
401	after the tool returns.
402	
403	Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
404	squashes → pushes):
405	
406	- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
407	  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
408	  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
409	  the invoke — into the snapshot.
410	- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
411	  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
412	  the bot to do more work.
413	- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
414	  after the tool returns.
415	
416	**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
417	finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
418	saying, say it *before* the invoke.
419	
420	### Recovery
421	
422	- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
423	  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
424	  finalize by hand:
425	
426	  ```
427	  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
428	  ```
429	
430	- **Run finalize again if `@` is non-empty** after the
431	  finalize's squash-and-commit (also desirable after
432	  extra activity by the bot's agents).
433	  - Why: finalize snapshots `.claude` at `--delay`, but the
434	    bot keeps writing session data while it runs — the tool's
435	    own invocation plus the bot's closing response land
436	    *after* the snapshot.
437	  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
438	    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
439	    change; it is not under the user's control.)
440	  - Fix: run `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push
441	    <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach` another time to capture
442	    that tail; one extra pass is generally enough.
443	    - Verified 0.64.0: two finalizes were executed and
444	      we purposely used the bot again and the second finalize
445	      captured everything.
446	  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
447	    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
448	    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
449	    and land after the snapshot. The remedy is the same: just
450	    run finalize again. This is why a single pass is never
451	    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
452	- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
453	  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
454	  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
455	  stale stage.
456	- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
457	  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
458	  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
459	  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
460	  push.
461	
462	## ochid trailers
463	
464	A **chid** is jj's change ID — a permanent identifier
465	that survives rebases and describes (see
466	[`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md)). An **ochid** trailer
467	on a commit body links it to its counterpart in the
468	other repo via that counterpart's chid, written as a
469	workspace-root-relative path. See
470	[`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) for the
471	multi-line trailer design (multi-user / forking).
472	
473	Path semantics — paths start with `/`, the workspace root
474	(the app repo); `/.claude` is the bot session sub-repo:
475	
476	- `ochid: /<chid>` references a change in the **app repo**.
477	- `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` references a change in the
478	  **`.claude` repo**.
479	
480	Trailers are blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the
481	end of the commit body, using the chid's **12-character**
482	prefix:
483	
484	```
485	ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqowktp   # points to a .claude change
486	ochid: /wtpmottvxqzl           # points to an app change
487	```
488	
489	How many, and which direction:
490	
491	- **Code-side commits** each carry one
492	  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
493	  change ID.
494	- **The `.claude` commit** has one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
495	  per code commit in that push. More than one occurs on
496	  Merge non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in
497	  the cycle).
498	
499	Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change
500	IDs (first line app, second `.claude`).
501	
502	### Resolvability
503	
504	A change ID travels with its commit: a **pushed** commit
505	resolves to the same chid in every clone — cloning the
506	`.claude` repo gave the published `main` tip the same chid
507	as an existing clone. The bot thinks jj carries the change
508	ID in the git commit object, so it survives
509	`jj git clone` / fetch.
510	
511	The local-only case is the **working-copy `@`**: jj mints a
512	fresh random chid for `@` in each clone, so an unpushed `@`
513	is never a stable ochid target. This is why a `.claude`
514	ochid names `@-` (the last committed change), not `@`.
515	
516	### .vc-config.toml
517	
518	Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its
519	location within the workspace, so tools resolve these paths
520	without repeating the workspace path in every trailer:
521	
522	```toml
523	# In vc-x1 (workspace root):
524	[workspace]
525	path = "/"
526	
527	# In .claude (sub-repo):
528	[workspace]
529	path = "/.claude"
530	```
531	
532	## Iterative work
533	
534	When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
535	from incremental review, loop:
536	
537	1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
538	2. Make the next round of changes.
539	3. User reviews the round (see
540	   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
541	4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
542	   new empty `@`.
543	5. If not done, go to step 2.
544	
545	Same jj mechanics as a
546	[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
547	single-commit scope — the cycle's version suffix
548	doesn't change.
549	
550	## Sub-cycle ladders
551	
552	When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
553	[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
554	commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
555	**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
556	parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
557	for review and bisection only.
558	
559	### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder
560	
561	For each Work commit in the ladder:
562	
563	1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
564	2. Do the commit's work.
565	3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable** — build
566	   and clippy alone miss regressions until a later
567	   commit runs the full suite, raising bisection cost.
568	4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
569	   only (no version suffix); the sub-cycle Close-out
570	   collects everything into one final commit with the
571	   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker.
572	
573	### Navigating the ladder
574	
575	Common moves:
576	
577	- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
578	  from its base.
579	- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
580	  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
581	- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
582	- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
583	  isolation.
584	
585	Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
586	descendants auto-rebase.
587	
588	### Close-out: squash the ladder
589	
590	When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:
591	
592	```
593	jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
594	```
595	
596	`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
597	keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
598	After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
599	intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.
600	
601	Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.
602	
603	For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
604	when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.
605	
606	### Recovery
607	
608	If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
609	prior commits:
610	
611	- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
612	  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
613	  parent.
614	- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
615	  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
616	  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
617	- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
618	  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
619	  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
620	  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.
621	
622	# References
623	
624	- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
625	  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
626	- [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) — multi-ochid
627	  trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
628	- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
629	  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
630	  for squash-recipe experiments.
631	- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-commit ladder usage. The
632	  per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
633	  after a regression introduced in an early commit wasn't
634	  caught until a later one ran the full suite.
635

file.content:
# Cycle protocol

This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form).

## Cycles

A cycle has three phases:

- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
  - Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.
  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
    statement + plan ladder).
  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
  needs; each runs through the
  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
    `## Done`.
  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
    [chores section](#chores-sections).
  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
    changed.

A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
result must always be published at close-out. See
[Pushing](#pushing).

**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
for the local-ladder mechanics.

## Chores sections

A **chores section** is a `##` section in
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle:

- One section per cycle is the default.
- Per-commit sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a
  deliberate alternative.

The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).

Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):

- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
  `Commits:` ref** — see
  [Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references).
- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
- **Update `Cargo.lock`** to match — run `cargo build`
  (or `cargo check`) so the lockfile's `vc-x1` version
  tracks `Cargo.toml` in the same commit.
- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
  - A **plan ladder**.
- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
  close-out title.

## Work-N

The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
implement the change. As many as needed:

- Each commit runs through the
  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
  visibility).
- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
  [Pushing](#pushing)).
- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
  scope grows enough (see
  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
what happens post-squash:

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
  placeholder opened during Preparation):
  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
    chores `##` header already carries that string).
  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
    back-reference.
  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
  - Replace `## In Progress` with
    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
see [Pushing](#pushing).

## Numbering

The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase —
the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

Disambiguation:

- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
  Preparation.
- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
  identifier `0`).

**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:

- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle

Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.

Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
reports the active phase at build time.

## Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
   for the loop-and-squash technique).
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
   cycle mandatory at close-out):
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   changes and we iterate until complete.
6. **Write the commit description** — see
   [Commit description](#commit-description).
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

**Two overrides apply:**

- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
  surface it; don't push through.
- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
  a review or question forward.

## Commit description

[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)
with a version suffix:

```
<type>: <short description> (<version>)
```

### Title

- ≤50 chars total (version suffix counts).
- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
  `docs`, `chore`.
- Favor terse phrasings.

### Body

[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:

- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
  files with multiple distinct changes:

  ```
  - path/to/file1
    - first distinct change
    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
       with continuation indented 5
  ```

  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.

- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
  in-session activity rather than code changes.

### Trailer

`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
[ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).

## Reviewing changes

Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:

- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev

Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
are in [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).

## Pushing

### Policy

Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
the cycle's result must be published.

### Shape at close-out push

At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
needs approval), so choose deliberately.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
  focused on one or two files.
- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
  for the full setup sequence.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.

### Merge non-ff recipe

Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
Work commit:

1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.

   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.

**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
push force-updates `main`.
The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
the close-out push.

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.

`vc-x1 push` injects the `ochid:` trailers itself (the
`commit-app` / `commit-claude` stages append them) — don't
hand-write them into the commit body or `--title`/`--body`.

**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
planned:

- N:1 code↔bot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).

### .claude cadence

**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
with every code commit in that push.

The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
pushes to the wrong remote ref.

Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
session information created, which we don't worry about.
The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
remain empty — this behavior was verified in 0.64.0.

### Bot communication at the reviews

Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
"Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
  --body ...` invocation by default.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.

### After push or finalize: stop and wait

After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
stop for the whole turn.

`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
closing words *before* invoking it; do not purposely emit anything
after the tool returns.

Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
squashes → pushes):

- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
  the invoke — into the snapshot.
- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
  the bot to do more work.
- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
  after the tool returns.

**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
saying, say it *before* the invoke.

### Recovery

- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
  finalize by hand:

  ```
  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
  ```

- **Run finalize again if `@` is non-empty** after the
  finalize's squash-and-commit (also desirable after
  extra activity by the bot's agents).
  - Why: finalize snapshots `.claude` at `--delay`, but the
    bot keeps writing session data while it runs — the tool's
    own invocation plus the bot's closing response land
    *after* the snapshot.
  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
    change; it is not under the user's control.)
  - Fix: run `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push
    <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach` another time to capture
    that tail; one extra pass is generally enough.
    - Verified 0.64.0: two finalizes were executed and
      we purposely used the bot again and the second finalize
      captured everything.
  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
    and land after the snapshot. The remedy is the same: just
    run finalize again. This is why a single pass is never
    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
  stale stage.
- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
  push.

## ochid trailers

A **chid** is jj's change ID — a permanent identifier
that survives rebases and describes (see
[`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md)). An **ochid** trailer
on a commit body links it to its counterpart in the
other repo via that counterpart's chid, written as a
workspace-root-relative path. See
[`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) for the
multi-line trailer design (multi-user / forking).

Path semantics — paths start with `/`, the workspace root
(the app repo); `/.claude` is the bot session sub-repo:

- `ochid: /<chid>` references a change in the **app repo**.
- `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` references a change in the
  **`.claude` repo**.

Trailers are blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the
end of the commit body, using the chid's **12-character**
prefix:

```
ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqowktp   # points to a .claude change
ochid: /wtpmottvxqzl           # points to an app change
```

How many, and which direction:

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
  change ID.
- **The `.claude` commit** has one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
  per code commit in that push. More than one occurs on
  Merge non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in
  the cycle).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change
IDs (first line app, second `.claude`).

### Resolvability

A change ID travels with its commit: a **pushed** commit
resolves to the same chid in every clone — cloning the
`.claude` repo gave the published `main` tip the same chid
as an existing clone. The bot thinks jj carries the change
ID in the git commit object, so it survives
`jj git clone` / fetch.

The local-only case is the **working-copy `@`**: jj mints a
fresh random chid for `@` in each clone, so an unpushed `@`
is never a stable ochid target. This is why a `.claude`
ochid names `@-` (the last committed change), not `@`.

### .vc-config.toml

Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its
location within the workspace, so tools resolve these paths
without repeating the workspace path in every trailer:

```toml
# In vc-x1 (workspace root):
[workspace]
path = "/"

# In .claude (sub-repo):
[workspace]
path = "/.claude"
```

## Iterative work

When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
from incremental review, loop:

1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
2. Make the next round of changes.
3. User reviews the round (see
   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
   new empty `@`.
5. If not done, go to step 2.

Same jj mechanics as a
[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
single-commit scope — the cycle's version suffix
doesn't change.

## Sub-cycle ladders

When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
for review and bisection only.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work.
3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable** — build
   and clippy alone miss regressions until a later
   commit runs the full suite, raising bisection cost.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
   only (no version suffix); the sub-cycle Close-out
   collects everything into one final commit with the
   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves:

- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
  from its base.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
  isolation.

Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.

Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.

For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.

# References

- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
- [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) — multi-ochid
  trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
  for squash-recipe experiments.
- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-commit ladder usage. The
  per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
  after a regression introduced in an early commit wasn't
  caught until a later one ran the full suite.

file.content:
close-out bookkeeping; one `vc-x1 push main`.

## fix: refuse ochid-dropping squash (0.65.2)

`finalize_exec` (`src/finalize.rs`) squashed with
`--use-destination-message` unconditionally, so a described
journal on `@` lost its message and `ochid:` trailers — this
broke the code↔session cross-links in the fc project (Bugs #1
in [bugs.md](../bugs.md)). Decision: refuse the squash when the
source message carries `ochid:` trailers the destination's
message lacks — the destination has its own ochids, so any
automatic message merge guesses wrong in some direction.

- The guard compares the two messages' column-0 `ochid:`
  trailers and errors when the source carries any the
  destination lacks, listing them with a by-hand remedy.
- It runs twice: in the parent's preflight (early, visible
  failure) and again in `finalize_exec` right before the
  squash, so a `jj describe` landing inside the `--delay`
  window is still caught.
- Adjacent hardening rode along: failure-marker surfacing
  moved to *after* the command's own output (with a
  historical banner) so a detached child's past failure
  isn't misread as the current run's, and the marker's
  `error=` value is flattened to keep one `key=value` per
  line.

### As-built ladder

Cycle used the Keep-separate shape — each rung pushed as its
own commit on `main` via `vc-x1 push`:

- 0.65.2-0 Preparation: backfill 0.65.1 Commits ref, bump
  version, pick up the entry, open this chores section.
- 0.65.2-1 ochid-trailer guard: `extract_ochids` /
  `ochids_at_risk` / `check_squash_keeps_ochids` + unit
  tests; wired into preflight and `finalize_exec`; README
  manual-test section; post-run marker surfacing;
  `support/gen-exmpl-1-3.sh` regenerator.
- 0.65.2 close-out: this bookkeeping commit.

### Outcome

The guard's three behaviors — synchronous refusal, normal
squash, and the detached-race re-check with a surfaced marker
— are demonstrated in the README's "Testing the ochid-trailer
guard" section, regenerable via `support/gen-exmpl-1-3.sh`.

# References

[4]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/28a0211a364a "28a0211a364aea03d19fc14a655275ba98c0498f"

input.new_string:
**feat: reposition @ onto synced bookmark (0.66.0)**

After `vc-x1 sync --no-check` fast-forwards a bookmark to the
updated remote, `@` is often left parented on the pre-fetch
tip. Add a final apply-mode pass that moves `@` onto the
freshly-synced bookmark, per-repo, only when it's safe —
folding in / replacing the current `ensure_at_on_main` rebase.

Rules (code repo: `xxx` = the synced `--bookmark`; `@-` =
parent of `@`):

- `xxx` a proper descendant of `@-`, `@` empty → `jj new xxx`.
- `xxx` a proper descendant of `@-`, `@` non-empty → rebase
  `@` onto `xxx`, gated by `--rebase` (else prompt / skip +
  inform).
- `xxx == @-` → already positioned, no-op.
- `xxx` not a descendant of `@-` → leave `@`, inform why.
- `.claude`: `@-` on `main` (ancestor-or-equal) →
  `jj new main`, else error.
- Reposition failures don't roll back the successful fetch /
  fast-forward — the pass sits outside the `op_restore` revert
  region.

Plan ladder:

- 0.66.0-0 Preparation (current) — backfill 0.65.2 Commits
  ref, bump to 0.66.0-0, pick up this entry, open the chores
  section.
- 0.66.0-1 scaffolding — `--rebase` bool on `SyncArgs` /
  `SyncParams`; bot-repo re-derivation + `@` / `@-` empty and
  ancestry probes; unit tests.
- 0.66.0-2 reposition pass — implement the code + `.claude`
  rules as a final apply-mode pass, outside the `op_restore`
  revert region; fold out `ensure_at_on_main`; integration
  tests (fast-forward, non-empty, diverged, `.claude`
  off-main error).
- 0.66.0 close-out — Done entry, move `## In Progress` →
  chores, README update for `--rebase`.

1. **sync: reposition @ onto the synced bookmark after a
   successful apply.** After `vc-x1 sync --no-check`
   fast-forwards a bookmark to the updated remote, `@` is
   often left parented on the pre-fetch tip. Add a final
   apply-mode pass that moves `@` onto the freshly-synced
   bookmark, per-repo, only when it's safe. Folds in / replaces
   the current `ensure_at_on_main` rebase step.
   - Runs only in apply mode (`--no-check`), as a final pass
     after every repo has synced without error; `--check`
     never mutates `@`.
   - Code repo (`xxx` = the synced `--bookmark`; `@-` = parent
     of `@`):
     - `xxx` a proper descendant of `@-`, `@` empty →
       `jj new xxx`.
     - `xxx` a proper descendant of `@-`, `@` non-empty →
       rebase `@` onto `xxx`, gated by `--rebase` (bool);
       absent → prompt `"@ has changes — rebase onto xxx?
       [y/N]"`, skip + inform if declined.
     - `xxx == @-` → already positioned; no-op.
     - `xxx` not a descendant of `@-` (diverged / `@` ahead)
       → leave `@`, inform why it didn't move (soft, no
       error).
   - `.claude` repo: `@-` on `main` (ancestor-or-equal) →
     `jj new main`; else error. No empty-check, no `--rebase`;
     with `.claude`'s content a conflict is very unlikely, and
     the user resolves one if it ever appears.
   - Reposition failures are reported *without* rolling back
     the already-successful fetch / fast-forward — the pass
     sits outside the `op_restore` revert region in
     `sync_repos`.
   - Plumbing: the repo list reaching `sync_repos` is
     `Vec<PathBuf>` with the `Code`/`Bot` role already
     collapsed (`resolve_params_to_repos` → `scope_to_repos`);
     re-derive the bot repo (`workspace_root.join(other-repo)`)
     to route the two rules.
   - New `--rebase` bool on `SyncArgs` / `SyncParams`; its only
     consumer is the code-repo non-empty branch.
   - The bot thinks the fast-forward is the motivating case:
     `@-` = old tip C1, `xxx` = new tip C2 descending from C1,
     so `jj new xxx` lifts a clean `@` to C2 and jj
     auto-abandons the old empty `@`.
2. **Version-number protocol is fragile — versions are

73	1. **Version-number protocol is fragile — versions are
74	   baked into titles/bodies/todo/done/chores before the
75	   change lands.** The cycle protocol embeds an `X.Y.Z-N`
76	   version in commit titles and bodies, `## Todo` /
77	   `## Done` entries, and chores headers — all written
78	   while the work is in progress, i.e. before it lands.
79	   But version numbers are subject to change: in a public,
80	   merge-based flow (e.g. Linux), the version a change
81	   ships under is only fixed when it merges into `main`,
82	   so the landing version can't be anticipated while the
83	   work is underway. Pervasive version-in-text is
84	   therefore fragile for any non-linear / multi-contributor
85	   workflow. Promoted from Ideas at 0.65.2-0; slated for
86	   the cycle after 0.65.2.
87	   - Open question: what identifies a cycle's commits if
88	     not a pre-assigned version?
89	     - Needs to be unique within some agreed upon domain.
90	       A contributors email address would do it, but also
91	       a UUID (short-version) for a contribution. I could
92	       imagine a UUID generated from the initial email/issue
93	       that and then "version number" schema appended to that.
94	   - Surfaces to update once the identifier is chosen:
95	     cycle-protocol.md (title shape, Numbering), AGENTS.md
96	     (commit-recording headers), and the `vc-x1` validators
97	     that parse `(X.Y.Z)` strings.
98	2. **validate-numbering: rename the pair, check all
99	   sequence-managed notes files generically.** `validate-todo`
100	   / `fix-todo` only operate on the single file passed, so a
101	   renumber slip in `bugs.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, or
102	   `todo.md`'s `## Ideas` section passes unnoticed — too weak
103	   for a pre-commit gate. Prereq for the pre-commit doc
104	   validators (Todo "pre-commit: single rule ...").
105	   - Rename the pair: `validate-todo` → `validate-numbering`,
106	     `fix-todo` → `fix-numbering` — they validate numbered-
107	     sequence integrity, not todos specifically.
108	   - Generic detection: for every `#…#` section, validate the
109	     column-0 `^\d+\.␠` entries form a contiguous 1..N run.
110	     Drops the Todo/Bugs special-casing; auto-covers
111	     `## Ideas` and any new numbered section. Keep the
112	     column-0 anchor so indented sub-lists aren't counted.
113	   - Default scope: a fixed list of sequence-managed notes
114	     files (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`) so the
115	     no-arg pre-commit run covers them all. Fixed rather than
116	     a `notes/**.md` walk because prose docs
117	     (`cycle-protocol.md`, design notes) carry ordinary
118	     numbered lists that aren't managed sequences — a walk
119	     would false-positive (markdown renders `1. 1. 1.` as
120	     1-2-3, a legitimate prose pattern).
121	   - Override args follow the `--init-from` convention:
122	     positional files/dirs (a dir → its `*.md`) plus an
123	     `@<file>` manifest, additive — for ad-hoc validation of
124	     a specific file.
125	   - Open: revisit fixed-vs-glob at implementation if the
126	     fixed list proves annoying to maintain.
127	3. **pre-commit: single rule (no docs skip) + doc validators.**
128	   The pre-commit (cargo cycle: fmt/clippy/test/install) only
129	   checks code, so it's "skip-able for purely-docs commits" —
130	   but that exception is exactly where checks slip (skipped on
131	   0.62.0-7/-8 until caught). And `vc-x1 push`'s `preflight`
132	   stage re-runs the same cycle, which invites treating push as
133	   the gate rather than a redundant safety-net.
134	   - Adopt one rule, no exception: the pre-commit runs before
135	     Work review on every commit; push's `preflight` is a
136	     safety-net, not the primary gate. (docs: AGENTS.md Cycle
137	     Protocol summary + cycle-protocol.md per-commit-flow.)
138	   - Enrich the pre-commit so it's meaningful on docs commits:
139	     add the doc validators — `validate-numbering` (its own

141	     both the documented flow and push's `preflight` stage
142	     (`push.rs`), with a test. (code)
143	   - This dissolves the docs exception: with doc validators in

145	     the carve-out stops making sense.
146	   - Its own near-term cycle (chosen over a 0.61.1 insert to
147	     avoid rewriting published 0.62.0-x history); no version
148	     pre-assigned — see the Todo "Version-number protocol is
149	     fragile" on fragile version targets.
150	4. **vc-x1 push: validate body opens with an intro paragraph.**
151	   A body whose first line is a bullet (`- file: …`) is a
152	   Prose-Form violation — bodies must open with an intro
153	   paragraph, then bullets. Today such a body trips jj's arg
154	   parser (`jj commit -m "<body>"` reads the leading `-` as a
155	   stray flag) and push fails with an opaque error. Hit on

157	   - Feature, not a parser bug (reframed): push should
158	     *validate* the body opens with a non-dash intro line and

content.text: Understood — keep-separate, `vc-x1 push main`. Let me confirm the exact flag names first:

content.content:
vc-x1 0.65.2

Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command.

Collapses today's manual Commit-Push-Finalize Flow into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-app → commit-claude (skipped if clean) → bookmark-both
→ push-app → finalize-claude. Failures in commit-app /
commit-claude / bookmark-both roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-app.
After push-app succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (positional form of `--bookmark`).
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Run fmt / clippy / test / install / retest

toolUseResult.stdout:
vc-x1 0.65.2

Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command.

Collapses today's manual Commit-Push-Finalize Flow into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-app → commit-claude (skipped if clean) → bookmark-both
→ push-app → finalize-claude. Failures in commit-app /
commit-claude / bookmark-both roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-app.
After push-app succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (positional form of `--bookmark`).
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Run fmt / clippy / test / install / retest
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate

content.content:
1	//! Integration tests for the sync module.
2	//!
3	//! End-to-end tests for `sync_repos` against real dual-repo jj
4	//! fixtures. Each test builds an isolated fixture (bare-git
5	//! remotes + colocated jj repos) under a unique tempdir via
6	//! `Fixture::new`, then drives the scenario with plain `jj`
7	//! subprocess calls. Requires `jj` in `PATH`.
8	//!
9	//! Fixtures clean themselves up via `Fixture`'s `Drop` impl so a
10	//! panicking test still removes its tempdir.
11	
12	use super::*;
13	use crate::options_flags::scope::Side;
14	use crate::test_helpers::Fixture;
15	use std::fs;
16	use std::process::Command;
17	
18	use crate::common::{default_scope, find_workspace_root_from, scope_to_repos};
19	
20	/// Resolver helpers must consume what `init --repo-local` produces.
21	///
22	/// Builds a real dual-repo fixture (bare git remotes + colocated jj
23	/// repos + the canonical `.vc-config.toml` pair init writes), then
24	/// drives the resolver chain `sync()` uses when `-R` is empty:
25	///
26	/// - `find_workspace_root_from(&fx.claude)` walks up to `fx.work`
27	///   (proves cwd-portability against init's `path = "/.claude"` /
28	///   `path = "/"` config split).
29	/// - `default_scope(Some(&fx.work))` reads the workspace config and
30	///   resolves to the dual-repo default.
31	/// - `scope_to_repos` maps each `Scope` shape to the right
32	///   absolute path(s) under the fixture.
33	///
34	/// Pure check on the resolver chain — does not invoke `sync()`
35	/// itself, since that walks `std::env::current_dir()` and parallel
36	/// `cargo test` makes cwd mutation unsafe.
37	#[test]
38	fn resolver_chain_against_init_repo_local() {
39	    let fx = Fixture::new("resolver-chain");
40	
41	    // Walk-up from the bot side lands on the app root.
42	    assert_eq!(
43	        find_workspace_root_from(&fx.claude).as_deref(),
44	        Some(&*fx.work),
45	        "find_workspace_root should resolve from .claude up to work"
46	    );
47	    // Walk-up from the app root finds itself.
48	    assert_eq!(
49	        find_workspace_root_from(&fx.work).as_deref(),
50	        Some(&*fx.work)
51	    );
52	
53	    // init --repo-local writes other-repo = ".claude", so the
54	    // workspace's default scope is dual.
55	    assert_eq!(
56	        default_scope(Some(&fx.work)),
57	        Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot])
58	    );
59	
60	    // Each scope shape resolves to the right absolute path(s).
61	    assert_eq!(
62	        scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap(),
63	        vec![fx.work.clone(), fx.claude.clone()]
64	    );
65	    assert_eq!(
66	        scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Code]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap(),
67	        vec![fx.work.clone()]
68	    );
69	    assert_eq!(
70	        scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Bot]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap(),
71	        vec![fx.claude.clone()]
72	    );
73	
74	    // sync_repos accepts the resolved list and reports up-to-date
75	    // — the resolver's output is shaped the way sync expects.
76	    let resolved = scope_to_repos(&Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot]), Some(&fx.work)).unwrap();
77	    sync_repos(&resolved, &apply_params()).expect("sync should succeed on resolved repos");
78	}
79	
80	/// Run `jj <args> -R <repo>` and assert success; returns trimmed stdout.
81	fn jj(repo: &Path, args: &[&str]) -> String {
82	    let out = Command::new("jj")
83	        .args(args)
84	        .arg("-R")
85	        .arg(repo)
86	        .output()
87	        .expect("spawn jj");
88	    assert!(
89	        out.status.success(),
90	        "jj {args:?} failed in {}: {}",
91	        repo.display(),
92	        String::from_utf8_lossy(&out.stderr)
93	    );
94	    String::from_utf8_lossy(&out.stdout).trim().to_string()
95	}
96	
97	/// Resolve `rev` to its short commit id in `repo`.
98	fn cid(repo: &Path, rev: &str) -> String {
99	    jj(
100	        repo,
101	        &["log", "-r", rev, "--no-graph", "-T", "commit_id.short(12)"],
102	    )
103	}
104	
105	/// Sync params with `--no-check` set (apply mode).
106	///
107	/// Integration tests pass explicit repo paths through `sync_repos`
108	/// directly, so `repo` / `scope` stay `None` here and the CLI-side
109	/// default resolution is not exercised by this helper.
110	fn apply_params() -> SyncParams {
111	    SyncParams {
112	        no_check: true,
113	        quiet: false,
114	        bookmark: "main".to_string(),
115	        remote: "origin".to_string(),
116	        repo: None,
117	        scope: None,
118	    }
119	}
120	
121	/// Add a local-only commit on `main` in `repo` (not pushed), then
122	/// restore `@` to an empty child so pre-flight still passes.
123	///
124	/// Sequence: write file → describe `@` → advance `main` to `@` →
125	/// create a fresh empty `@` above it.
126	fn add_local_commit(repo: &Path, file: &str, content: &str, msg: &str) {
127	    fs::write(repo.join(file), content).expect("write local file");
128	    jj(repo, &["describe", "@", "-m", msg]);
129	    jj(repo, &["bookmark", "set", "main", "-r", "@"]);
130	    jj(repo, &["new"]);
131	}
132	
133	/// Clone `remote_url` into `<base>/<work_name>`, add a commit, push it.
134	///
135	/// Used to make the remote advance beyond the fixture's `main`
136	/// from a separate working copy. Returns the pushed commit's id.
137	fn push_from_clone(
138	    base: &Path,
139	    remote_url: &Path,
140	    work_name: &str,
141	    file: &str,
142	    content: &str,
143	    msg: &str,
144	) -> String {
145	    let workdir = base.join(work_name);
146	    let out = Command::new("jj")
147	        .args(["git", "clone", "--colocate"])
148	        .arg(remote_url)
149	        .arg(&workdir)
150	        .output()
151	        .expect("spawn jj clone");
152	    assert!(
153	        out.status.success(),
154	        "jj git clone failed: {}",
155	        String::from_utf8_lossy(&out.stderr)
156	    );
157	    fs::write(workdir.join(file), content).expect("write remote file");
158	    jj(&workdir, &["describe", "@", "-m", msg]);
159	    jj(&workdir, &["bookmark", "set", "main", "-r", "@"]);
160	    jj(&workdir, &["git", "push", "--bookmark", "main"]);
161	    cid(&workdir, "main")
162	}
163	
164	/// Scenario 1: fresh fixture, nothing to do — `sync` leaves both
165	/// repos untouched.
166	#[test]
167	fn sync_up_to_date() {
168	    let fx = Fixture::new("up-to-date");
169	    let work_main = cid(&fx.work, "main");
170	    let claude_main = cid(&fx.claude, "main");
171	    sync_repos(&fx.repos(), &apply_params()).expect("sync should succeed");
172	    assert_eq!(cid(&fx.work, "main"), work_main);
173	    assert_eq!(cid(&fx.claude, "main"), claude_main);
174	}
175	
176	/// Scenario 2a: a non-empty `@` on top of main (simulates `/exit`
177	/// trailing session writes in `.claude`) is tolerated when there's
178	/// nothing new on the remote. `@`'s commit id changes because jj
179	/// snapshots the written file, but its content is preserved and
180	/// `@` stays reachable from main.
181	#[test]
182	fn sync_tolerates_trailing_at_up_to_date() {
183	    let fx = Fixture::new("trailing-uptodate");
184	    let pre_main = cid(&fx.claude, "main");
185	    fs::write(fx.claude.join("trailing.jsonl"), "{\"line\":1}\n").expect("write trailing file");
186	    sync_repos(&fx.repos(), &apply_params()).expect("sync should succeed");
187	    assert_eq!(cid(&fx.claude, "main"), pre_main, "main should not move");
188	    let on_main = jj(
189	        &fx.claude,
190	        &[
191	            "log",
192	            "-r",
193	            "main::@",
194	            "--no-graph",
195	            "-T",
196	            r#"commit_id.short(12) ++ "\n""#,
197	        ],
198	    );
199	    assert!(!on_main.trim().is_empty(), "@ should stay on main's line");
200	    assert_eq!(
201	        fs::read_to_string(fx.claude.join("trailing.jsonl")).unwrap(),
202	        "{\"line\":1}\n",
203	        "trailing content preserved"
204	    );
205	}
206	
207	/// Scenario 2b: `@` has trailing writes and the remote advanced
208	/// while the session was offline. jj's fetch auto-ff's main but
209	/// leaves `@` behind; sync must then rebase `@` onto the new main
210	/// so the session tail doesn't end up orphaned. Content is
211	/// preserved across the rebase.
212	#[test]
213	fn sync_rebases_trailing_at_when_main_moves() {
214	    let fx = Fixture::new("trailing-rebase");
215	    let remote_claude = fx.base.join("remote-claude.git");
216	    let remote_head = push_from_clone(
217	        &fx.base,
218	        &remote_claude,
219	        "claude2",
220	        "remote.md",
221	        "remote\n",
222	        "feat: remote-added",
223	    );
224	    // Trailing writes on @
225	    fs::write(fx.claude.join("trailing.jsonl"), "{\"line\":2}\n").expect("write trailing file");
226	    sync_repos(&fx.repos(), &apply_params()).expect("sync should succeed");
227	    assert_eq!(
228	        cid(&fx.claude, "main"),
229	        remote_head,
230	        "local main should match remote after auto-ff"
231	    );
232	    // @ should now be a descendant of main.
233	    let on_main = jj(
234	        &fx.claude,
235	        &[
236	            "log",
237	            "-r",
238	            "main::@",
239	            "--no-graph",
240	            "-T",
241	            r#"commit_id.short(12) ++ "\n""#,
242	        ],
243	    );
244	    assert!(
245	        !on_main.trim().is_empty(),
246	        "@ should be reachable from main after ensure_at_on_main"
247	    );
248	    // Trailing content preserved on disk.
249	    assert_eq!(
250	        fs::read_to_string(fx.claude.join("trailing.jsonl")).unwrap(),
251	        "{\"line\":2}\n",
252	        "trailing content preserved across @ rebase"
253	    );
254	}
255	
256	/// Scenario 2c: `@` has trailing writes and local+remote both
257	/// modify the same file differently. Rebase produces conflicts;
258	/// sync fails and op-restores both repos. The trailing content
259	/// must survive the revert — jj documents that op restore rolls
260	/// back operation history without clobbering the working copy.
261	#[test]
262	fn sync_conflict_preserves_trailing_at_on_revert() {
263	    let fx = Fixture::new("trailing-conflict");
264	    let remote_claude = fx.base.join("remote-claude.git");
265	    push_from_clone(
266	        &fx.base,
267	        &remote_claude,
268	        "claude2",
269	        "shared.txt",
270	        "remote-version\n",
271	        "feat: remote shared",
272	    );
273	    // Local commit on main with conflicting content
274	    add_local_commit(
275	        &fx.claude,
276	        "shared.txt",
277	        "local-version\n",
278	        "feat: local shared (conflicting)",
279	    );
280	    // Trailing writes on new @
281	    fs::write(fx.claude.join("trailing.jsonl"), "{\"line\":3}\n").expect("write trailing file");
282	
283	    let pre_main = cid(&fx.claude, "main");
284	    let pre_remote = cid(&fx.claude, "main@origin");
285	
286	    let err = sync_repos(&fx.repos(), &apply_params())
287	        .unwrap_err()
288	        .to_string();
289	    assert!(
290	        err.contains("conflicts"),
291	        "expected conflict error, got: {err}"
292	    );
293	
294	    // Post-revert: main and main@origin back to pre-sync.
295	    assert_eq!(cid(&fx.claude, "main"), pre_main, "main reverted");
296	    assert_eq!(
297	        cid(&fx.claude, "main@origin"),
298	        pre_remote,
299	        "main@origin reverted"
300	    );
301	    // No conflicts remain.
302	    let conflicts = jj(
303	        &fx.claude,
304	        &[
305	            "log",
306	            "-r",
307	            "conflicts()",
308	            "--no-graph",
309	            "-T",
310	            r#"commit_id ++ "\n""#,
311	        ],
312	    );
313	    assert!(
314	        conflicts.trim().is_empty(),
315	        "no conflicts should remain after revert"
316	    );
317	    // Trailing content preserved on disk.
318	    assert_eq!(
319	        fs::read_to_string(fx.claude.join("trailing.jsonl")).unwrap(),
320	        "{\"line\":3}\n",
321	        "trailing content preserved across op restore"
322	    );
323	}
324	
325	/// Scenario 3: local has commits not yet pushed; sync classifies
326	/// `ahead` and leaves the local bookmark alone even under
327	/// `--no-check`.
328	#[test]
329	fn sync_ahead_is_noop() {
330	    let fx = Fixture::new("ahead");
331	    add_local_commit(&fx.work, "local.txt", "local\n", "feat: local only");
332	    let ahead_head = cid(&fx.work, "main");
333	    sync_repos(&fx.repos(), &apply_params()).expect("sync should succeed");
334	    assert_eq!(cid(&fx.work, "main"), ahead_head);
335	}
336	
337	/// Scenario 4: clean divergence — both sides advance main on
338	/// different files; sync rebases local onto remote and the result
339	/// is conflict-free.
340	#[test]
341	fn sync_diverged_rebases() {
342	    let fx = Fixture::new("diverged");
343	    let remote_code = fx.base.join("remote-code.git");
344	    let remote_head = push_from_clone(
345	        &fx.base,
346	        &remote_code,
347	        "work2",
348	        "remote.txt",
349	        "remote\n",
350	        "feat: remote only",
351	    );
352	    add_local_commit(&fx.work, "local.txt", "local\n", "feat: local only");
353	
354	    sync_repos(&fx.repos(), &apply_params()).expect("sync should succeed");
355	
356	    // Remote tracking bookmark now points at the pushed remote commit.
357	    assert_eq!(
358	        cid(&fx.work, "main@origin"),
359	        remote_head,
360	        "main@origin should match pushed remote commit"
361	    );
362	    // Local main is ahead of remote — rebased local commit sits on top.
363	    let main_after = cid(&fx.work, "main");
364	    assert_ne!(
365	        main_after, remote_head,
366	        "local main should be ahead of remote after rebase"
367	    );
368	    // Remote is an ancestor of local post-rebase.
369	    let anc = jj(
370	        &fx.work,
371	        &[
372	            "log",
373	            "-r",
374	            &format!("{remote_head}::{main_after}"),
375	            "--no-graph",
376	            "-T",
377	            r#"commit_id.short(12) ++ "\n""#,
378	        ],
379	    );
380	    assert!(
381	        !anc.trim().is_empty(),
382	        "remote should be ancestor of local after rebase"
383	    );
384	    // No conflicts.
385	    let conflicts = jj(
386	        &fx.work,
387	        &[
388	            "log",
389	            "-r",
390	            "conflicts()",
391	            "--no-graph",
392	            "-T",
393	            r#"commit_id ++ "\n""#,
394	        ],
395	    );
396	    assert!(conflicts.trim().is_empty(), "no conflicts expected");
397	}
398	
399	/// Scenario 5: conflicting divergence — both sides modify the
400	/// same path differently. Rebase produces conflicts; sync fails
401	/// and reverts both repos to their pre-sync state.
402	#[test]
403	fn sync_diverged_conflict_reverts() {
404	    let fx = Fixture::new("conflict");
405	    let remote_code = fx.base.join("remote-code.git");
406	    push_from_clone(
407	        &fx.base,
408	        &remote_code,
409	        "work2",
410	        "shared.txt",
411	        "remote-version\n",
412	        "feat: remote shared",
413	    );
414	    add_local_commit(
415	        &fx.work,
416	        "shared.txt",
417	        "local-version\n",
418	        "feat: local shared (conflicting)",
419	    );
420	
421	    let pre_main = cid(&fx.work, "main");
422	    let pre_remote = cid(&fx.work, "main@origin");
423	
424	    let err = sync_repos(&fx.repos(), &apply_params())
425	        .unwrap_err()
426	        .to_string();
427	    assert!(
428	        err.contains("rebase produced conflicts"),
429	        "unexpected error: {err}"
430	    );
431	
432	    // After revert: main and main@origin are back where they started.
433	    assert_eq!(cid(&fx.work, "main"), pre_main, "main should be reverted");
434	    assert_eq!(
435	        cid(&fx.work, "main@origin"),
436	        pre_remote,
437	        "main@origin should be reverted (pre-fetch state)"
438	    );
439	    // No conflicts remain.
440	    let conflicts = jj(
441	        &fx.work,
442	        &[
443	            "log",
444	            "-r",
445	            "conflicts()",
446	            "--no-graph",
447	            "-T",
448	            r#"commit_id ++ "\n""#,
449	        ],
450	    );
451	    assert!(
452	        conflicts.trim().is_empty(),
453	        "no conflicts should remain after revert"
454	    );
455	}
